msContents.summary: Funerary text written for the Nespeheran, a priest ("god's father") of the god Amun consisting of twenty-three petitions to twenty-three deities with each petition accompanied by a representatiton of the particular deity addressed.

supportDesc.extent: A complete papyrus roll written along the fibres with the back being blank. The papyrus is mounted on paper and housed in four glass cases. The original papyrus scroll measures 2440 240

layoutDesc.layout: Written in 23 columns with an illustration following each unit of text.

handDesc.handNote: Likely written by one scribe, all in black ink in "Book of the Dead cursive".

decoDesc.decoNote: A series of illustrations are included in the text. The first frame starts with the representation of the soul ("ba") of the deceased Nespeheran, its head surmounted with a dab of scented ointment and a lotus bud; in front a lighted lamp, this is followed by the first deity addressed, repsresented as a long snake coiling in the frame called Seneh-her; the second image showsa disk containing a ram's head with an uraeus snake between its horns called "radiance"; the third one shows a bird in a sun-disck with a cat below called the Soul of Ra; fourth represents a lion- or panther-headed god, called Netet efem neket. The second frame starts with an image showing a mummiform divinity with a head of a griffin, called Lord of Strength; this is followed by a crocodile-headed divinity with averted face, called "turned face"; this is followed by a mummiform figure with a a burning lamp as its head called the "Great Heat", the next image shows a goat seated on its haunches in a mock-human position called the Ram"; the penultimate image shows a mummiform divinity with the head of bubale called "he whose body is hidden"; the last image is a mummiform deity with a bearded ram's head called "The Great Flood";. The third frame starts with an image of a deity in the form of a captive, kneeling on one knee with his arms bound behind his backcalled the "Vanquished One"; the next image is a godess with two heads, the one of a lioness, the other of a crocodile called Saryt; the next one shows a ram-headed beetle, surmounted by the solar disk between two uraeus snakes called the "Venerated Power"; the next one is an ox-headed mummiform deity named the "Bull of hte West"; the penultimate one shows the sacred eye in a circle called the "Divine Eye",; the last one shows a mummiform deity called the "Hidden One". the foruth frame starts with the image of mummiform divinity with a donkey's head, who grasps a gecko-lizard with both hands called "Mysterious of Modes of being", this is followed by a lion-headed mummiform divinity with a live snake in his mouth called the "Hidden One"; the next one is another mummiform divinity, whose had is a pot issueing water called "Divine Water"; this is followed by another mummified figure with the head of a hare called "he who lets his voice resound"; this is followed by another mummiform figure with a basket of onions and bread as its head, called "Lord of Bread"; the penultimate image is a snake-headed mummiform god called the Righteous One" and the last one is a lion-like cat seated on its haunches called "Cat of Lapis Lazuli".

history.provenance: Possibly from Thebes. Purchased by Colonel Valentine Patrick Terrell Vivian in Egpt around 1882 and bequeathed to his brother-in-law the Reverend Vivian Eccles Skrine.
history.acquisition: Given by the Reverend Vivian Eccles Skrine (1850-1925), 30 March 1912.

recordHist.source: Summary description abbreviated from the following sources: Blackman, Aylward M., “The Funerary Papyrus of Nespeḥer'an (Pap. Skrine, No. 2),” Journal of Egyptian Archaeology 5.1 (1918), 24–35.

availability.p: This item may only be consulted by prior arrangement with a curator. To make an application, please click the request button above. When your request is received, you will be asked to contact the relevant person.

listBibl.head: Printed descriptions:
listBibl.bibl: Blackman, Aylward M., “The Funerary Papyrus of Nespeḥer'an (Pap. Skrine, No. 2),” Journal of Egyptian Archaeology 5.1 (1918), 24–35.
